2003-01-01.title: U.S. Ties With Seoul Strained by Troop Presence and North Korea
2003-01-01.abstract: After 50 years as a stalwart U.S. ally, South Korea is now one of the administration's biggest foreign policy problems.

2003-01-01.title: Mexican Officials Report Threats by Zapatista Rebels
2003-01-01.abstract: Zapatista rebels are stirring on eve of ninth anniversary of their armed uprising against Mexican government and its free-trade accord with United States; North American Free Trade Agreement eliminates tariffs on most agricultural goods on Jan 1; across Mexico, people fear that flood of cheaper, government-subsidized American exports will ruin hundreds of Mexican farmers (M)

2003-01-01.title: Russia to Shut Monitoring By Europeans In Chechnya
2003-01-01.abstract: Russia will shut down mission in Chechnya of Organization for Security and Cooperation in Europe, ending any permanent international monitoring in republic after mission's mandate ends tonight (M)

2003-01-01.title: World Briefing: Europe
2003-01-01.abstract: TURKEY: Change Opens Door For Popular Politician; BOSNIA: U.N. Cedes Police Role To Europe; BRITAIN: Deal On London Tube Overhaul; ITALY: Volcano Aftermath On Isle; FRANCE: Effects Of Spanish Spill;.

2003-01-01.title: ARMY ACTS TO ADD THOUSANDS OF G.I.'S TO ITS GULF FORCE
2003-01-01.abstract: US Army acts to send thousands more soldiers from Third Infantry Division in Georgia to Kuwait in largest single ground deployment to Persian Gulf since war there in 1991; one of division's three combat brigades, about 4,000 soldiers, has been training in Kuwaiti desert since September, but unit's headquarters at Fort Stewart, Ga, receives order within last day directing rest of more than 15,000 combat troops to join soldiers in Kuwait; photo (M)

2003-01-02.title: China Cracks Down on Protests After Sending Dissident to U.S.
2003-01-02.abstract: Chinese authorities intensify efforts to suppress protesters in country's restive northeast, just week after releasing Xu Wenli, country's most prominent political prisoner, and sending him to United States; police charge two leaders of worker demonstrations in city of Liaoyang with subversion, crime that carries death penalty, after they organize protests to focus attention on workers' interests; China's decision to release Xu, who has pushed for greter democracy in China since late 1970's and has spent 16 of past 21 years in prison, was greeted as significant concession to US and Western critics of its human rights record; but Western rights groups warned that releases of prominent dissidents are mixed blessing, often leading to fresh crackdowns on less-well-known protesters (M)

2003-01-02.title: Inquiry Into Attacks in Yemen Suggests Wider Plot, Official Says
2003-01-02.abstract: The investigation into recent attacks in Yemen suggests a cell of Islamic militants has been planning to target other foreigners as well.

2003-01-02.title: International Herald Tribune Now Run Solely by The Times
2003-01-02.abstract: International Herald Tribune appears for first time under sole ownership and management of The New York Times Co; takeover ends 35-year partnership between The Times and The Washington Post that produced journalistic hybrid consisting mainly of articles and editorials from both papers compiled by editors in Paris; The Times bought The Post's 50 percent stake for $70 million; beginning Jan 2, contents of The Tribune will come increasingly from staff of The Times, with continued contributions by international paper's own small staff and dwindling number of articles from The Post and other newspapers in United States; international paper's name and appearance will stay same, and its headquarters will remain in Neuilly-sur-Seine, suburb of Paris; Howell Raines, executive editor of The Times, will oversee news operations of The Tribune (M)

2003-01-02.title: China Planning a Manned Space Mission for 2003
2003-01-02.abstract: China plans to launch a manned capsule into orbit around Earth by the end of the year, official media reported today.

2003-01-02.title: U.S. to Let Mexico Sell 'Dolphin Safe' Tuna
2003-01-02.abstract: The United States will permit Mexico to sell tuna under the "dolphin safe" label, ending a 12-year-old embargo.

2003-01-03.title: North Korea Defends Decision to Re-Start Nuclear Program
2003-01-03.abstract: North Korea's top diplomat in Beijing blamed the U.S. for his country's decision to re-start its nuclear program.

2003-01-03.title: For Israeli Schoolchildren, Lessons in Gas Masks as Well as Geography
2003-01-03.abstract: Israeli children get lessons at school on donning gas masks in case of Iraqi attack in response to US military action against Saddam Hussein; photo of instruction in fourth-grade classroom in Jerusalem; psychologist Dr Bilha Noy observes that children in Israel lose some of their innocence in having to prepare for such events (M)

2003-01-03.title: North Korea Defends Decision to Restart Nuclear Program
2003-01-03.abstract: North Korea's top diplomat in Beijing blamed the U.S. for his country's decision to restart its nuclear program.

2003-01-03.title: Bashiqa Journal; A Sect Shuns Lettuce and Gives the Devil His Due
2003-01-03.abstract: Bashiqa Journal on Iraqi village of Yazidis, sect of ethnic Kurds who follow eclectic faith that mingles Islam, Christianity and Zoroastrianism, along with some obscure practices like fierce prohibition on eating lettuce; surrounding hills are dominated by spired conical tombs of sect that suffered ruthless persecution under Ottomans; photos; map; armies and empires have crisscrossed Iraq for thousands of years, leaving remnants of spectacular civilizations and people who seem to date from long forgotten time (M)

2003-01-03.title: Iraq Says U.N. Teams Have Found No Weapons
2003-01-03.abstract: Iraq says UN weapons inspectors who have visited some 230 sites have thus far failed to find any weapons of mass destruction or evidence of other prohibited programs (S)

2003-01-04.title: The Poor Woman Is Gone And Her Castle's Besieged
2003-01-04.abstract: Local authorities in Wales plan to take Cardigan Castle and turn it into museum and tourist attraction now that its owner, Barbara Wood, who has jealously kept them at bay, is moved to retirement home; lawyer who claims to have power of attorney over her affairs refuses to hand property over and has offered it for sale for $1.28 million; photos (M)

2003-01-04.title: World Briefing | Europe: Britain: Officers Face Child Sex Charges
2003-01-04.abstract: Two British police officers are charged with possessing and distributing indecent images of children, and one is charged with indecent assault on two girls under 16 (S)

2003-01-04.title: Small Crowds Of Pakistanis Stage Protests Against U.S.
2003-01-04.abstract: Thousands of people in six Pakistani cities protest against possible American attack on Iraq at rallies organized by coalition of hard-line religious parties; rallies are relatively modest by Pakistani standards; photo (M)

2003-01-04.title: Drug firms accused of devising women's malady
2003-01-04.abstract: PARIS:—  The British Medical Journal touched off a major controversy Friday when it published an article charging that pharmaceutical companies had invented a new condition called female sexual dysfunction so they could create a vast new market for drugs to treat it.

2003-01-04.title: New Seoul Leader to Press U.S. and North to Yield a Bit
2003-01-04.abstract: Pres-elect Roh Moo Hyun of South Korea is preparing compromise proposal that would require both North Korea and US to accept concessions to resolve standoff over North's nuclear program; North Korea renews its call for US to reopen talks with it, but US State Department says North Korea must first stop its two programs to build nuclear bombs; photo (M)

2003-01-05.title: White House Wary of Cuba's Little Spy Engine That Could
2003-01-05.abstract: New debate about whether Cuba is threat to American national security is set off by recent discoveries by federal investigators of Cuban espionage activities, including operation that penetrated Pentagon's intelligence agency; since 1988, FBI has exposed network of at least 16 accused or convicted Cuban spies who succeeded in penetrating several exile groups and worked to get inside American military installations; success appears to have been mixed; debate pits administration officials who want to pressure Castro government against bipartisan Congressional leaders who are trying to ease restrictions on Cuba; recent spy cases cited; intelligence officials say Cuba might still have as many as several hundred officers and agents operating in US; contend they are among most aggressive intelligence agents; photo (M)

2003-01-05.title: Finland's Bold Musical Ambitions Produce a Multitude of Maestros
2003-01-05.abstract: Finland's Sibelius Academy has launched startling number of conductors who have gone on to prestigious podiums around world; country provides large subsidies for education for composers and performers; photos (M)

2003-01-05.title: Kuwaiti Political Combatants Hold Breath for Iraq Outcome
2003-01-05.abstract: Political factions in Kuwait are carefully monitoring looming possibility of war next door, and weighing what overthrow of Saddam Hussein and refashioning of Iraq would mean for competing reformist and Islamic trends at home; photo (M)

2003-01-05.title: In Iraq's Tribes, U.S. Faces a Formidable Wild Card
2003-01-05.abstract: Intensely devout, armed and nationalistic tribes of Iraq are under increased scrutiny as Bush administration casts about for some credible force that can help it oust Saddam Hussein; country is home to about 150 major tribes, with largest numbering more than million people and smallest a few thousand; question now is how deep their professed loyalty to Hussein runs; map; photo (M)

2003-01-05.title: Venezuelan Minister Says Chávez Won't Give In to Strikers
2003-01-05.abstract: Venezuelan Foreign Min Roy Chaderton accuses strike leaders of shutting down heart of nation's economy in effort to overthrow government, but regrets violence that erupted during recent march against Pres Hugo Chavez; news reports says two men were killed when protest turned into street fight that lasted most of day; Chaderton says Chavez is trying to prevent repeat of last April's coup attempt when he was forced by military to leave office after violent demonstrations; photo (M)

2003-01-06.title: Pair of Bombers Kill 23 in Israel; Reprisals Begin
2003-01-06.abstract: Two suicide bombers commit deadliest attack against Israel in 10 months, blowing themselves up just seconds apart in downtown area of Tel Aviv; 23 are killed and 100 more injured; area is crowded with foreign laborers and Sunday evening shoppers; spokesman for Al Aksa Martyrs Brigades, Palestinian militant organization, claims responsibility; Israeli reprisals begin, with gunships entering skies over Gaza and rocketing Palestinian positions there; Prime Min Ariel Sharon's cabinet meets in emergency session; rescue teams encounter scene of horrific carnage; say two bombs appear to be unusually large and were packed with nails and ball bearings and hunks of metal to sharpen their effect; photos; map (M)

2003-01-06.title: Inspectors Close Exits At Research Site for Hours, Confining Thousands
2003-01-06.abstract: United Nations weapons inspectors in Iraq make dramatic use of their authority, closing exits and entrances to any site where they are working and confining thousands of people at sprawling government research complex, including Iraqi ambassador to UN, for almost six hours; Amb Mohammed Aldouri, fuming with anger, calls inspection team's action 'unjustified' and says they should have acted in more civilized way; events underscore way both sides are stepping up pressure in dwindling time before first official assessment in inspectors' work; photos (M)

2003-01-06.title: Kenya's New Government Begins Revamping Policies
2003-01-06.abstract: Kenya's new government began revamping policies today, starting with public education and past human rights violations.

2003-01-06.title: 3 Terror Suspects Agree to Extradition to U.S.
2003-01-06.abstract: Three men sought accused of plotting to trade drugs for antiaircraft missiles agreed today to their extradition.

2003-01-06.title: Thousands March at Rites for 2 Slain in Clashes in Venezuela
2003-01-06.abstract: Streets of Caracas become scene of mass funeral procession as thousands of supporters of Pres Hugo Chavez denounce violence stemming from monthlong strike aimed at forcing him from power; marchers escort coffins of two men killed in clashes between government opponents and pro-Chavez groups; Vice Pres Jose Vicente Rangel and several other high-level government officials lead procession; fear of civil war grows; photo (M)

2003-01-07.title: Primary Schools in Kenya, Fees Abolished, Are Filled to Overflowing
2003-01-07.abstract: Kenya's new Pres Mwai Kibaki immediately acts to fulfill campaign pledge to make primary schools free; students find overflowing classrooms in some parts of country as parents who could not afford to send their children to class under old policy take advantage of new rules; some schools are unable to enroll all who seek to register; authorities agree to take down names of those who are left out and to help them enroll next year; photo (M)

2003-01-07.title: 3 in Hong Kong Agree to Face Charges in U.S.
2003-01-07.abstract: Three men sought by FBI for allegedly plotting to trade drugs for antiaircraft missiles agree to their extradition to United States from Hong Kong; men reportedly told FBI undercover agents in Hong Kong that they hoped to resell Stinger missiles to Al Qaeda; men, two Pakistanis and an American, do not say why they have dropped their opposition to extradition, but officials in Hong Kong have stessed they would have no tolerance for terrorism; men are Ilyas Ali, Syed Saadat Ali Faraz and Muhammad Abid Afridi (M)

2003-01-07.title: World Briefing: Europe
2003-01-07.abstract: BRITAIN: Crackdown On Gun Possession; NORTHERN IRELAND: Bomb Found At School;.

2003-01-07.title: Tokyo Journal; Hot New Marketing Concept: Mall as Memory Lane
2003-01-07.abstract: Shopping mall geared to older shoppers is big hit in Tokyo, Japan, doing booming business in otherwise flat economy; it defies practice of letting young set trends in retailing, offering displays of old-fashioned toys and catering to nostalgia for optimistic era when country was booming; photos (M)

2003-01-07.title: Israel Won't Let Palestinian Leaders Attend London Talks
2003-01-07.abstract: Israel's leaders forbid Palestinian leaders to leave country for talks in London, as much of rest of Israel takes day to mourn its dead following pair of deadly suicide attacks; talks in London, being sponsored by Prime Min Tony Blair, are intended to encourage Palestinian leadership to become more democratic and less corrupt; Israeli officials also order closing of three Palestinian universities; Israeli tanks and helicopters continue military operations in Gaza Strip, where they say they killed one Palestinian gunman; photos; map (M)

2003-01-08.title: World Briefing: Asia
2003-01-08.abstract: PAKISTAN: Religious Coalition Seeks Islamic Law; CHINA: Amusement At U.S. Charges; JAPAN: Premier Vows To Press Reforms;.

2003-01-08.title: World Briefing | Europe: France: More Jews Leaving For Israel
2003-01-08.abstract: Number of Jews in France who moved to Israel more than doubled last year to 2,566, highest number since 1972; most are between 16 and 35 and have college degrees; anti-Semitic attacks in France cited, such as recent stabbing of Rabbi Gabriel Farhi (S)

2003-01-08.title: Environment And Science: Danes Rebuke A 'Skeptic'
2003-01-08.abstract: Danish scientific panel concludes that Prof Bjorn Lomborg displayed 'scientific dishonesty' in popular book The Skeptical Environmentalist, which offers upbeat analysis of environmental trends; Lomborg says book is unbiased refutation of dire pronouncements by environmental groups; many experts say conditions are not as good as Lomborg says, but also not as bad as some environmentalists claim (M)

2003-01-08.title: Britain Calls Up 1,500 Reservists For Possible Action Against Iraq
2003-01-08.abstract: Britain calls up 1,500 reservists to join active troops for possible military action against Iraq; Sec Geoff Hoon also announces reinforcing of naval task force in Persian Gulf; Prime Min Tony Blair tells gathering of British diplomats that standing by US is 'massively' in British self-interest; photo; says price of influence is not leaving US to face 'tricky issues alone' and denounces anti-Americanism as 'foolish indulgence'; pledges at same time to urge Bush administration to broaden agenda, saying active pursuit of Mideast peace is essential to winning Arab support against Saddam Hussein; Pres Jacques Chirac tells French troops to stand by, saying UN weapons resolutions must be enforced, but also warns US against precipitate action; more US war planners leave US Central Command in Tampa for Qatar; Blair urges Israeli Prime Min Ariel Sharon to reconsider barring Palestinians from traveling to London meeting; ban was ordered after suicide bombings killed 22 people in Tel Aviv (M)

2003-01-08.title: A Spiffy Army of Volunteers Parades in Praise of Hussein
2003-01-08.abstract: Thousands of Iraqi men and women who have just completed basic military training march through provincial capital of Baquba to show support for Saddam Hussein; map; parade follows bellicose Hussein speech denouncing UN arms inspectors as spies (M)

2003-01-09.title: World Briefing | Americas: Chile: Ex-Aide To President Faces Bribe Charge
2003-01-09.abstract: Chilean judge orders detention of former minister Carlos Cruz, longtime associate of Pres Ricardo Lagos, and two other officials on bribery charges (S)

2003-01-09.title: World Briefing: Asia
2003-01-09.abstract: INDIA-PAKISTAN NUCLEAR COMPETITION; SRI LANKA: Some Progress In Talks;.

2003-01-09.title: Sharon Rebuffed Appeal on Palestinian Talks, Britain Says
2003-01-09.abstract: LONDON, Jan. 9 — Britain said today that Israeli Prime Minister Ariel Sharon had rebuffed Prime Minister Tony Blair's personal appeal to reconsider Israel's decision to keep Palestinian negotiators from attending a British-sponsored conference in London next week. A spokesman for Mr. Blair vowed that some form of talks would go ahead as scheduled despite the Israeli ban.

2003-01-09.title: Iraq Gave No New Evidence in Arms Report, Inspector Says
2003-01-09.abstract: Hans Blix said today that Iraq's report was "devoid of any new evidence" that the country had no banned weapons.

2003-01-09.title: Huge Demonstration in China, but Subject Is Traffic Safety
2003-01-09.abstract: Some 10,000 people took the streets in the eastern city of Hefei this week to protest problems with traffic safety.

2003-01-10.title: U.S. and China Agree to Work Together on North Korea
2003-01-10.abstract: President Bush conferred with President Jiang Zemin today on North Korea's decision to withdraw from an arms treaty.

2003-01-10.title: World Briefing | Africa: Rwanda: Detainees To Be Freed
2003-01-10.abstract: Rwanda plans to release up to 40,000 detainees who have been held for years for reported involvement in 1994 mass killings (S)

2003-01-10.title: Spanish Premier's Wife Becomes Candidate
2003-01-10.abstract: Spanish Prime Min Jose Maria Aznar's wife, Ana Botella, announces her candidacy in Madrid's municipal election on ticket of governing Popular Party; she was offered No. 3 slot by mayoral candidate for Madrid, Alberto Ruiz-Gallardon, who has been mentioned as possible successor to Aznar when his term is up in 2004 (S)

2003-01-10.title: U.N. Inspectors Criticize Iraqis Over Arms List
2003-01-10.abstract: Chief United Nations weapons inspectors sharply criticize Iraq for failing to come forward with new information to clarify its weapons program, but say they have 'not found any smoking gun' indicating that Baghdad is concealing illegal weapons; American officials seize on inspectors' report as new proof that Iraq is not cooperating with inspections as required by Security Council resolutions; American diplomats suggest they will step up pressure at UN for military action if Iraq does not change by end of month; Hans Blix, one of two chief inspectors, says declaration Iraq presented in December is lengthy but lacks much new information; says Baghdad has given 'inadequate response' to demand for complete list of Iraqi weapons experts; Dr Mohamed ElBaradei, director general of International Atomic Energy Agency, says aluminum tubes Baghdad tried to import were intended to build rockets and not centrifuges to enrich uranium; Bush administration has cited tubes as central piece of evidence for its claim that Pres Saddam Hussein is trying to restart nuclear arms program; inspections chiefs cast serious doubt on Iraq's cooperation so far; photo (M)

2003-01-10.title: World Briefing | Asia: India: Plans For Dual Citizenship
2003-01-10.abstract: Indian Prime Min Atal Bihar Vajpayee says government plans legislation to permit dual citizenship for Indians in United States, Britain, Canada, Australia, New Zealand and Singapore (S)

2003-01-11.title: Unexpected Appointment in Gov. Richardson's Datebook
2003-01-11.abstract: Bill Richardson, newly sworn in as New Mexico governor, takes on unexpected role in effort to defuse nuclear confrontation involving North Korea at request of Han Song Ryol, one of North Korea's United Nations envoys; two have known each other for years; Richardson, who has visited North Korea in past as negotiator, stresses he acts unofficially, passing along data to State Sec Colin L Powell; makes clear that he does not ignore state problems; photo (M)

2003-01-11.title: G.O.P. Leaders Promise Repeal Of Provisions Hidden in Bill
2003-01-11.abstract: Republican congressional leaders say Congress will eliminate three special-interest provisions that caused furor when they were introduced anonymously into domestic security law last year; one limits liability of vaccine makers, idea that Sen Bill Frist says he still supports; second allows contracts with US companies that move overseas to avoid taxes; third creates domestic security research center in way that gives preference to Texas institutions (M)

2003-01-11.title: U.S. Official to Discuss Trade; Africa Hopes to Discuss AIDS
2003-01-11.abstract: United States Trade Rep Robert B Zoellick is expected to face street protests and officials concerned about American policies on generic versions of patented AIDS drugs and farm subsidies when he visits southern Africa to discuss free trade accords; African officials are upset that Pres Bush scrapped plans to attend Africa Trade and Economic Cooperation Forum, Mauritius, and is instead sending junior cabinet member (M)

2003-01-11.title: Venezuela Crisis Complicates Iraq Situation, Experts Say
2003-01-11.abstract: United States officials and industry experts say strike that has virtually shut down Venezuela's oil industry is creating major complications for Bush administration's drive to oust Iraqi Pres Saddam Hussein, causing oil shortages that would probably make war in Iraq more costly to economy than expected; officials say distrust of American diplomats hampers efforts to end stalemate between Pres Hugo Chavez and his opponents; crisis could be compounded if Chavez follows through on proposal to split government-owned oil company, Petroleos de Venezuela SA, into two parts and restructure its central offices; US sees him seeking to replace managers and union leaders with political loyalists, making company less efficient, cutting output and making US even more dependent on Middle Eastern oil; graph of US petroleum imports; photos (M)

2003-01-11.title: Korea Crisis: Same Song, Second Verse
2003-01-11.abstract: North Korea's nuclear arms crisis echoes nation's 1993-94 threat to pull out of Nuclear Nonproliferation Treaty; United States response to threat recalled (S)

2003-01-12.title: Sharon Sees Political Motives in Arafat's Appeal
2003-01-12.abstract: The Israeli leader clearly implied today that Yasir Arafat's recent cease-fire call was an attempt to influence Israel's Jan. 28 elections.

2003-01-12.title: India Harvests Fruits of a Diaspora
2003-01-12.abstract: India welcomes nearly 2,000 Indians who emigrated or whose forebears emigrated for conference intended determine how resources and achievements of Indian diaspora might be used to uplift India; legislation is being introduced to grant dual citizenship to people of Indian origin living in US, Britain, Australia, New Zealand, Canada and Singapore; participants voice concerns ranging from Hindu-Muslim violence to Indian government's silence when rights of Indians abroad are violated; map shows world distribution of nonresident Indians; photos (M)

2003-01-12.title: Germans Arrest 2 Yemenis at U.S. Request
2003-01-12.abstract: German authorities arrest two Yemeni men in Frankfurt at US request; main suspect is identified as Sheik Muhammad Ali Hassan al-Mouyad, imam at mosque in Sana, whom American officials describe as significant Qaeda figure and fund-raiser for Osama bin Laden; Yemeni officials react with surprise; photos; map (M)

2003-01-12.title: Afghan Editors Test Freedom's Boundaries With Cartoons
2003-01-12.abstract: Editors and journalists in Afghanistan face risks if they push boundaries of political satire even though Pres Hamid Karzai has announced that press is free; Abdul Ghafoor Iteqad, editor of Kabul weekly Farda, was jailed for two nights on order of defense minister, Marshal Muhammad Fahim, on ground that one of its cartoons insulted Karzai; Karzai, who was out of country at time, ordered Iteqad's release upon his return; cartoon (M)

2003-01-12.title: U.S. Woman Is Released From Prison In Indonesia
2003-01-12.abstract: Joy Lee Sadler, American nurse who went to Indonesia as sort of freelance Florence Nightingale, is released from prison after having been convicted by Indonesian authorities of contacting separatist rebels in Aceh province; will return to home in Waterloo, Iowa, but wants to continue her one-person health mission; her companion, Lesley McCulloch, British academic, is due to be released in February; photo (M)

2003-01-13.title: U.S. Envoy Starts Discussions in Seoul on North Korea
2003-01-13.abstract: Asst Sec of State James A Kelly begins consultations with South Korean Pres-elect Roh Moo Hyun and other officials in Seoul about nuclear threat from North Korea; some developments in US and Russia indicate diplomatic solution might be bit closer, even as North Korean government issues barrage of bellicose statements; Gov Bill Richardson of New Mexico, who talked with North Korean representatives, says he expects low-level talks to begin between US and North Korea at United Nations; Russian Foreign Ministry expresses some optimism about possible settlement of crisis; photo (M)

2003-01-13.title: U.S. and Britain Assert No Deadline on Iraq Inspections
2003-01-13.abstract: The White House said today that the U.S. was "not putting any artificial timetable" on U.N. arms inspectors in Iraq.

2003-01-13.title: Sharon Says Arafat's Truce Call Aims at Influencing Israeli Voters
2003-01-13.abstract: Prime Min Ariel Sharon of Israel speaks dismissively of Yasir Arafat's appeal for pre-election halt to Palestinian attacks on Israeli civilians; implies that Arafat is trying to help his dovish opponent; Palestinian officials, who view Sharon as implacable enemy, have openly voiced their preference for his opponent Haifa Mayor Amram Mitzna, former general who has said he would dismantle Jewish settlements in West Bank and Gaza Strip and reopen direct negotiations with Palestinians (M)

2003-01-13.title: At a Reunion in Moscow, Tales of Economic Change
2003-01-13.abstract: First graduating class of Russia's New Economics School gathers for its 10th reunion in Moscow; Western-style graduate school was established in first year of new Russia; its students were first to receive Western-style graduate education in economics after Soviet Union fell; its graduates have grown into some of country's most promising young leaders; 34 graduates of first class are now in jobs in business, education and government; they have brought fresh ideas and outlooks to Russia, and now are powerful force for change; photo (M)

2003-01-13.title: Pope Expresses Opposition to Potential War in Iraq
2003-01-13.abstract: Pope John Paul II expressed his strongest opposition yet to a potential war in Iraq today, describing it as a "defeat for humanity."

2003-01-14.title: Another Step in France's Official Recognition of Muslim Leaders
2003-01-14.abstract: President Jacques Chirac rewarded those Muslim leaders who chose to join a council to represent an official Islam for France.

2003-01-14.title: U.S. Expects Iraqi Experts Will Be Taken Abroad for Questioning
2003-01-14.abstract: Bush administration expects international inspectors will try to bring Iraqi scientists and engineers out of country starting next week to interview them about weapons programs; officials say interviews may be conducted on Cyprus or at United Nations facilities in Europe; talks are being timed to get information from scientists before reports to UN by chief weapons inspectors Hans Blix and Mohamed ElBaradei (M)

2003-01-14.title: World Briefing | Europe: Vatican: Pope Plans Croatia Trip
2003-01-14.abstract: Pope John Paul II will travel to Croatia in early June (S)

2003-01-14.title: Clearing of Illinois Death Row Is Greeted by Cheers Overseas
2003-01-14.abstract: Reaction around world is almost unanimous in support of decision of departing Gov George Ryan of Illinois to pardon 4 death row inmates and commute sentences of 167 others; International Commission of Jurists, representing judges and senior lawyers in 60 nations, says it 'emphatically' supports decision (M)

2003-01-14.title: British Court Charges 4 Men As Terrorists; Arrests Spread
2003-01-14.abstract: Four Algerians accused of plotting attacks using lethal toxin ricin are formally charged with terror and chemical-weapons offenses in London court, as antiterror police arrest six more people in Bournemouth on English Channel; six are charged under Terrorism Act of 2000, aimed at those suspected of possessing 'articles of value to a terrorist'; Prime Min Tony Blair says arrests show there is very real threat to people of Britain (M)

2003-01-15.title: British Officer Slain, 4 Hurt As Terror Suspects Are Seized
2003-01-15.abstract: British police officer is stabbed to death during arrests of three terrorist suspects in Manchester; four other officers are wounded in raid linked to discovery of deadly poison ricin in London flat occupied by Algerians; six people arrested in Bournemouth are found to have been involved in terrorism hoax and possible immigration violations (M)

2003-01-15.title: World Briefing | Europe: Russia: Rebels Accused Of Killing Civilians
2003-01-15.abstract: Vladimir Kravchenko, Chief Prosecutor of Chechen administration, accuses guerrillas of kidnapping and then blowing up 10 civilians; dismembered bodies have reportedly been found across several acres near Grozny; victims have not been publicly identified and officials offer no proof as of yet to support their assertions (S)

2003-01-15.title: Lost in Afghanistan (Maybe): Bin Laden (Still) and 2 Others
2003-01-15.abstract: US and Afghan officials warn of continuing threat from Osama bin Laden, Taliban leader Mullah Muhammad Omar and warlord Gulbuddin Hekmatyar, who have frustrated more than year of efforts to find them; photos; they are believed hiding in remote mountains and lawless tribal areas straddling Pakistan border; UN officials report former Taliban members crossing back into Afghanistan (M)

2003-01-15.title: Britain's Hunt for Child Pornography Users Nets Hundreds Besides Pete Townshend
2003-01-15.abstract: More than 1,300 people have been arrested in Britain in last ten months as part of Operation Ore, nationwide hunt for users of child pornography; list includes police officers, judges and politicians, teachers and social workers, as well as Who guitarist Pete Townshend, whose computers were seized in London; Townshend says he suspects he was abused as child and was researching subject; detainees are all accused of downloading explicit photos from pay-per-view Web sites that contained hard-core pornography and would have been impossible to access by chance; case stems from US Postal Service probe of ring run by Janice and Thomas Reedy (M)

2003-01-15.title: Pentagon Official Wants to Speed Rebuilding of Afghanistan
2003-01-15.abstract: Paul D. Wolfowitz, the deputy defense secretary, outlined a new plan for using American forces to improve security.

2003-01-16.title: World Briefing | Europe: Cyprus: Leaders Hold Talks
2003-01-16.abstract: Turkish Cypriot leader Rauf Denktash and Greek Cypriot leader Glafcos Clerides have face-to-face meeting and plan additional meetings to discuss UN plan to reunite island after thirty-year division; effort had failed in earlier UN-led talks at European Union summit, but pending union membership for Cyprus has fueled renewed efforts toward unity; photo (S)

2003-01-16.title: World Briefing | Africa: Zambia: A Shady Ex-Official
2003-01-16.abstract: Former Zambian Finance Minister Katele Kalumba is arrested in connection with disappearance of $33 million dollars while he was in office; police found him hiding in tree near his home; several other past officials have been charged in corruption investigation in which police anticipate arrest of former President Frederick Chiluba (S)

2003-01-16.title: For Berlin and Paris, a facade
2003-01-16.abstract: PARIS:—  Jacques Chirac says that France and Germany's approach and vision concerning Iraq are "identical and of the same nature" — an elastic, credulity-stretching description of unity across the Rhine. It turns a wish into a single will, a country that describes its armed forces as ready for action into the supposed psychic mirror of a neighbor that rules out any role in a military strike against Saddam Hussein.

2003-01-16.title: Bush Says He Will Ask Congress To Extend Africa Trade Benefits
2003-01-16.abstract: Pres Bush says he will ask Congress to postpone expiration of trade law that gives 35 African nations duty-free access to American markets (M)

2003-01-16.title: Mexico City Journal; Enter Consultant Giuliani, His Fee Preceding Him
2003-01-16.abstract: Rudolph W Giuliani ends his first working tour of Mexico City, for which his firm is acting as crime-fighting consultant; many citizens and police officers do not expect him to come up with great ideas, but they are impressed at his $4.3-million fee, which could pay starting salaries of 1,400 police officers for a year; fee was paid by businessmen; photos (M)

2003-01-17.title: WAR DISSENT IN CHICAGO
2003-01-17.abstract: Chicago City Council votes 41 to 1 for resolution opposing unilateral, pre-emptive strike on Iraq, joining more than two dozen cities nationwide that have taken similar action (S)

2003-01-17.title: South Korea's President-Elect Rejects Use of Force Against North Korea
2003-01-17.abstract: Pres-elect Roh Moo Hyun of South Korea insists on peaceful approach toward North Korea, which he says wants to escape its status as rogue state, interview; will not contemplate military strike from South Korean territory against North Korean nuclear complex on Yongbyon, even if North Korea were found to be reprocessing nuclear fuel for bomb production there; tries to play down recent wave of anti-Americanism in South Korea and denies his candidacy rode such a wave; vows to keep his country's relationship with US strong while trying to usher North Korea out of international isolation; photo (M)

2003-01-17.title: World Briefing | Europe: Europe: Ban On Animal Tests For Cosmetics
2003-01-17.abstract: European Parliament approves law banning use of most animal tests to develop cosmetic products in European Union by 2009; also banned would be imports of cosmetics that have been tested on animals (S)

2003-01-17.title: Pentagon Draws Up a 20-to-30-Year Antiterror Plan
2003-01-17.abstract: Top US military officers draw up comprehensive plan to combat terror that includes confronting countries that sponsor terrorism, even if that would not necessarily mean direct attack; experts say applying pressure on Syria and Iran, Hezbollah's patrons, would likely be more effective than trying to attack Hezbollah itself; document, approved by Gen Richard B Myers, chairman of Joint Chiefs of Staff, represents shift to pre-emptive action against hostile states and terrorist groups; also addresses military's heightened role in homeland security (M)

2003-01-17.title: Japan's Monarchy, Once Godlike, Shows Its Frailty
2003-01-17.abstract: Announcement that Emperor Akihito has prostate cancer and will undergo surgery is significant development in Japan, where imperial family lives closely guarded existence; Akihito has been eager to put more mortal face on often-distant family; photo of people lining up at Imperial Place in Tokyo to add their prayers for Emperor Akihito; photo (M)

2003-01-18.title: Senate Panel Quickly Backs Ridge for New Antiterror Post
2003-01-18.abstract: Senate Governmental Affairs Committee unanimously approves nomination of Tom Ridge to be nation's first secretary of homeland security; some lawmakers are skeptical of his assertion that nation is 'far safer' now than it was before Sept 11, 2001, terrorist attacks; new department under his leadership will draw together 22 federal agencies with nearly 170,000 employees; photo (M)

2003-01-18.title: World Briefing | Americas: Mexico: Army Raids Drug Agents' Office
2003-01-18.abstract: Mexican army raids offices of drug enforcement officers in 11 states to seize evidence of enforcers' wrongdoing (S)

2003-01-18.title: Briefly Noted
2003-01-18.abstract: BUSH VISITS WOUNDED SOLDIERS President Bush visited five soldiers who had been wounded in Afghanistan, thanking them for their service and pledging to provide the military with the best possible care. Mr. Bush traveled to Walter Reed Army Medical Center in Washington for a 40-minute visit with the soldiers, all of whom suffered blast or shrapnel wounds. Afterward, he described the five, who were not identified, as

2003-01-18.title: Officials Press Ahead With Smallpox Shots
2003-01-18.abstract: Federal health officials say that government's smallpox vaccination program will proceed as planned despite concerns about lack of safeguards raised in expert advisory group's report and calls for delay by unions representing health care workers; Institute of Medicine report, written as confidential draft at request of federal Centers for Disease Control and Prevention, contains 23 recommendations and was obtained and first described by New York Times on Jan 16; federal program calls for vaccination of up to 500,000 health care workers in coming weeks; second phase calls for innoculating 10 million more people; eleven states are ready to begin vaccinations which are part of biodefense program, not public health program, and, unlike other innoculations, offer risk but no benefits to recipients in absence of attack, since smallpox has been eradicated (M)

2003-01-18.title: World Briefing | Europe: Iceland: Sharpening The Harpoons
2003-01-18.abstract: Icelandic Prime Minister David Oddsson visits Japan; says he wants his country to follow Japan's lead and start hunting whales for scientific research; Iceland announced plans to resume commercial whaling in 2006 (S)

2003-01-19.title: Warlord Is Said to Be Ready to End Standoff With Kabul
2003-01-19.abstract: Padsha Khan Zadran, powerful Afghan warlord who is pro-American and anti-Taliban but who opposes American-backed government of Pres Hamid Karzai, may be softening his opposition after months of deadlock and bloodshed; negotiations between Zadran and Karzai, through mediator, may be close to ending standoff; United Nations officials are seeking to negotiate face-saving way for Zadran to change in his position; he has railed against Karzai's 'illegitimate government,' but he is losing support as backing for Karzai grows; photo; map (M)

2003-01-19.title: Swiss Sign Pact to Curb the Rise Of Political Asylum Requests
2003-01-19.abstract: Less than two months after voters narrowly rejected an initiative that would have given Switzerland the most restrictive asylum laws in Europe, the Swiss government has signed an agreement aimed at curbing frivolous applications for political asylum.     The agreement with Senegal, signed last week in Dakar, Senegal's capital, allows Switzerland to deport to Senegal any West African whose asylum application has been rejected and whose country of origin is not clear.

2003-01-19.title: Chávez Set Back on Expanding Mediation Group
2003-01-19.abstract: Pres Hugo Chavez of Venezuela fails to win backing from Pres Luiz Inacio Lula da Silva of Brazil to include Russia, France and other 'friendly' nations in United States-sponsored mediation effort to restore peace to Venezuela; Chavez is facing growing demands from opponents at home to resignas economy buckles under sevn-week-long strike; he insists that mediation group be broadened; it currently includes US, Brazil Mexico, Spain, Portugal and Chile (M)

2003-01-19.title: Distrust Reopens the Door for Polio in India
2003-01-19.abstract: Polio virus--almost vanquished worldwide thanks to cheap and widely available vaccine--makes defiant comeback in India, borne along by rumor and fear; India had 1,509 newly diagnosed cases last year; vast majority are in Uttar Pradesh, country's most populous state, and one of its poorest; government officials and community leaders attribute this to widely believed rumor that oral vaccine is part of government population control scheme; mothers are refusing doses for their children, even when vaccinators come to their homes, fearing it will make their children sterile; polio outbreak has exposed religious health divide; majority of polio cases last year in Uttar Pradesh were among Muslims, although rumor was repeated in Hindu communities too; health officials note that Muslims in Uttar Pradesh tend to be landless laborers with lower literacy rates and greater mistrust of Hindu-dominated government; resurgence of polio in India alarms international health experts, who had aimed for global eradication by 2002; polio is now found in seven countries and has increased only in two: Nigeria and India; photos; map; chart (L)

2003-01-19.title: Fearful Saudis Seek a Way to Budge Hussein
2003-01-19.abstract: Saudi Arabian leadership seeks Saddam Hussein's removal as leader of Iraq as part of war-avoidance strategy, even as it signals Washington that it will cooperate extensively with American military buildup in Persian Gulf, including offering use of crucial bases and airspace; other Arab and Muslim states could join effort as Turkey seeks to assemble Iraq's neighbors for urgent discussions in Ankara, with explicit agenda of averting military conflict; Prime Min Abdullah Gul of Turkey says he has encouraged Hussein to step down, while senior Saudi intelligence officer is said to be in talks with Hussein's son Qusay on proposal to offer amnesty to Saddam Hussein along with exile home for members of his extended family; Iraqi officials deny that such talks are under way; many Arab leaders appear to believe that any option that removes Hussein without resorting to war is far superior to destructive military campaign (M)

2003-01-20.title: Study Urges More Action to Cut Risks From Weapons Stockpiles
2003-01-20.abstract: Study by consortium of 15 influential private research centers concludes that US, Russia and Europe should do far more to reduce urgent and 'grave proliferation risks' from their remaining stockpiles of nuclear, biological and chemical weapons; says US and Europe must begin treating Russia as partner in such efforts rather than as strategic charity case; says Russia must become more open and remove obstacles to cooperation to prevent world's most lethal arms and technology from spreading to rogue states andterrorist groups; was financed by Carnegie Corp of New York and Nuclear Threat Initiative (M)

2003-01-20.title: In the Street, Across Europe, a Weekend of Antiwar Rallies
2003-01-20.abstract: Rallies against US-led war against Iraq are held across Europe; world religious leaders meeting at Vatican appeal to believers in all faiths to work to avert conflict in Iraq; photo (M)

2003-01-20.title: EXILE FOR HUSSEIN MAY BE AN OPTION, U.S. OFFICIALS HINT
2003-01-20.abstract: Defense Sec Donald H Rumsfeld, Sec of State Colin L Powell and Condoleezza Rice, Pres Bush's national security adviser, hint that they would consider allowing Saddam Hussein to find safe haven from Iraq if that would avoid war, but they reject calls for delay before confronting him militarily; say moment of decision is fast approaching on whether Hussein's government has complied with disarmament demands from United Nations (M)

2003-01-20.title: Antiglobalization Forum to Return to a Changed Brazil
2003-01-20.abstract: World Social Forum, antiglobalization alternative to World Economic Forum, is set to open in Porto Alegre, capital of Brazilian state of Rio Grande do Sul, where voters have thrown out state's Workers Party government and elected one that embraces globalization and will try to attract multinational corporations that Workers Party shunned; map (M)

2003-01-20.title: U.S. Expands Aid Offer, and North Korea Wants to Talk
2003-01-20.abstract: US and North Korea move slightly closer toward resolution of crisis over nuclear weapons development with American offers of economic cooperation and North Korean calls for dialogue; photo (M)

2003-01-21.title: Mexico Challenges U.S. on Its Citizens on Death Row
2003-01-21.abstract: The government of Mexico asked the International Court of Justice today to block the executions of 51 Mexicans on death row in the U.S.

2003-01-21.title: World Briefing | Europe: Macedonia: Peace Role For Europeans
2003-01-21.abstract: Macedonian government requests European Union take over peace-keeping mission run by NATO since Yugoslav-Albanian uprising in 2001; action represents first use of European Union's new foreign and security policy; union anticipates replacement of NATO in Bosnia in next year (S)

2003-01-21.title: Avalanche Kills 8 U.S. Skiers Touring Canada
2003-01-21.abstract: Eight backpacking American sky mountaineers on helicopters tours of Selkirk Mountains of western Canada are buried and killed by avalanche (S)

2003-01-21.title: Blair Says Military Forces in Gulf Are Undermining Hussein
2003-01-21.abstract: Prime Minister Tony Blair told members of Parliament today that the escalation of armed forces is shaking Saddam Hussein.

2003-01-21.title: Libya to Head Rights Panel Despite U.S. Opposition
2003-01-21.abstract: UN Human Rights Commision, meeting in Geneva, elects Libya to chairmanship after US demands and loses vote (S)

2003-01-22.title: NATO Rebuffs U.S. in Debate Over Aid for a War With Iraq
2003-01-22.abstract: NATO postponed a decision on an American request for military assistance, mostly defensive in nature, that Washington said would be needed in the event of war with Iraq.

2003-01-22.title: Japan to Cut Contribution To the Budget Of the U.N.
2003-01-22.abstract: Japan, second largest contributor to UN, will cut support by one-quarter in coming years; country accounts for 14.4 percent of global economy and pays 19.5 percent of UN budget, whereas US accounts for 30 percent of world gross domestic product and pays 22 percent of UN costs; Japanese are also angered at not getting permanent seat on Security Council(M)

2003-01-22.title: Labor Government Unveils Finance Plans for Higher Education
2003-01-22.abstract: The Labor government proposed that universities should be allowed to charge students higher tuition fees, and that students, in turn, should be able to delay paying until after graduation.

2003-01-22.title: Iraqis Ambivalent on Inspections
2003-01-22.abstract: Iraqi citizens express anger mixed with hope towards the new U.N. mission to find weapons of mass destruction.

2003-01-22.title: Russians Say Times Report Is Untrue
2003-01-22.abstract: Russian Foreign Intelligence Service denies The New York Times report that Russian agents worked with CIA in early 1990's to monitor North Korean nuclear program (S)

2003-01-23.title: Dutch Appear to Re-elect Conservative Leader in Mixed Outcome
2003-01-23.abstract: Preliminary returns give 44 of 150 seats in Dutch Parliament to Christian Democratic Party of Prime Min Jan Peter Balkenende, but Labor recovers from drubbing it received in May 2002 by winning 42 seats; big loser is Pim Fortuyn List, down to 8 seats from 26 in May; formation of coalition may take weeks; mood of nation has clearly shifted to right (M)

2003-01-23.title: World Briefing | Europe: Yugoslavia: Pressure From U.S. On War Crimes
2003-01-23.abstract: Pierre-Richard Prosper, United States primary diplomat for war crimes, meets with high ranking officials, including President Vojislav Kostunica, to levy ultimatum that Serbia arrest and hand over Ratko Mladic and Veselin Sljivancanin by March 31 or face possible suspension of US aid; Mladic stands accused of genocide in Bosnia and Sljivancanin of crimes in Croatia (S)

2003-01-23.title: Many Iraqis, Like Their Government, Complain About Arms Inspections
2003-01-23.abstract: Iraqis complain of disruption and damage caused by international arms inspectors, even while they hope that war may be averted if inspectors complete their work without turning up chemical, biological or atomic weapons; inspectors are accepted in Iraq as wedge against any American attack, and they are also used to attack Bush administration; photo (M)

2003-01-23.title: NATO Secretary General to Leave His Post in December After 4 Years
2003-01-23.abstract: Lord Robertson says he will step down as NATO secretary general in December, resisting requests by many members to stay on for another year (M)

2003-01-23.title: Chaos, or Democracy, in Iraq Could Be Unsettling to Saudis
2003-01-23.abstract: Possible chaos from breakdown of existing order in Iraq has become overarching fear among ruling members of Saudi royal family, who realize success in ousting Pres Saddam Hussein could send uncertain winds of change sweeping through region; has motivated day-to-day ruler Crown Prince Abdullah to press efforts to motivate Pres Bush to acquiesce in 11th-hour strategy in which decision to attack Iraq would be followed by pause for intensive diplomacy, even coup-making, to remove Pres Saddam Hussein; Saudi Arabia's failed efforts of past 40 years to unite disparate tribes of peninsula and create sense of nationhood discussed; country has existed in state of arrested political development, under monarchy anchored in deeply conservative Islamic ideology that represses women's rights and excoriates foreign 'infidels;' suffers from extensive corruption, arising from enormous oil wealth (M)

2003-01-24.title: Defectors Bolster U.S. Case Against Iraq, Officials Say
2003-01-24.abstract: Bush administration officials have concluded that international inspectors are unlikely to find tangible and irrefutable evidence that Iraq is hiding weapons of mass destruction, so administration is preparing its own assessment that will rely heavily on evidence from Iraqi defectors; former Iraqi scientists, military officers and contractors have provided American intelligence agencies with portrait of Saddam Hussein's secret programs to develop and conceal chemical, biological and nuclear weapons that is starkly at odds with findings so far of UN weapons inspectors; but there are deep divisions in Washington over value of information from defectors; administration says three or four thought to have most valuable firsthand information have been placed in witness protection program and offered asylum (M)

2003-01-24.title: Europeans Try to Stem Anti-U.S. Anger
2003-01-24.abstract: Defense Sec Donald H Rumsfeld's dismissal of France and Germany as 'old Europe' because they are trying to avert war with Iraq outrages both European politicians and ordinary citizens, who take remark as example of America's growing arrogance (S)

2003-01-24.title: World Briefing | Asia: Japan: More Mad Cow Cases
2003-01-24.abstract: Two new cases of mad cow disease are diagnosed in Japan, bringing total infected to seven; suspiciously, six of those were weaned on formula imported from Western Europe in 1996; Japan supressed June 2001 report stating herds were at risk (S)

2003-01-24.title: World Briefing | Asia: China: Magnate Gunned Down
2003-01-24.abstract: Wealthy Chinese businessman Li Haicang is found dead in his Yuncheng office, apparently shot in head by old friend Feng Yinliang, who then shot himself; according to New China News Agency, motive appears to be tied to Feng's failed business and Li's refusal to help (S)

2003-01-24.title: Indonesian Educators Ponder Lessons After U.S. Visit
2003-01-24.abstract: Twenty-five educators from traditional Islamic schools in Indonesia spend month in United States as part of program sponsored by State Dept to help Muslims overseas learn about United States; goal is to show skeptical Indonesian Muslims that America is land of tolerance and diversity; Indonesians visited schools and universities, learned about democracy and met with State Dept's senior officials; they also visited Statue of Liberty and Empire State Building and went shopping; one of participants says he found Americans to be friendly, but he is distrustful of policies of American government; in Feb, television studio audience in Jakarta will talk with audience in US, and results will be shown later in both countries; photo; map (M)

2003-01-25.title: Spain Arrests 16 Suspected of Ties to Al Qaeda
2003-01-25.abstract: Prime Min Jose Maria Aznar says Spanish police officials have arrested 16 militants suspected of links to Al Qaeda, breaking up two cells and confiscating electronic material and containers of unidentified chemicals; antiterrorist experts say that Spain, with its historic and geographical ties to North Africa, has been used as logistic and financial base by groups linked to Al Qaeda; photo (M)

2003-01-25.title: Rival Palestinian Groups Meet in Cairo to Discuss Ending Attacks on Israeli Civilians
2003-01-25.abstract: Palestinian factions opened talks here today that their Egyptian hosts hoped would result in a cease-fire halting attacks against Israeli civilians.    But few, if any, in the Arab world or in Israel think there is much likelihood that the dozen rival groups can reach a joint position on the conduct of the increasingly bitter 28-month intifada, let alone on stopping suicide bombers inside Israel.

2003-01-25.title: Ridge Is Sworn In as a New Cabinet Secretary
2003-01-25.abstract: Department of Homeland Security opens for business with swearing in of Tom Ridge as its first secretary; skeleton staff will take control of 22 existing federal agencies over next several months, redirecting their mission to focus on defense of American soil from possibility of new terrorist attacks; Ridge declares 'we have a long way to go' in meeting goal of pre-empting new wave of terrorist strikes; Vice Pres Dick Cheney conducts swearing-in ceremonies with Pres Bush; photo (M)

2003-01-25.title: A Mother's Bitter Choice: Telling Kidnappers No
2003-01-25.abstract: Angelina Atyam has faced an awful trauma, and an awful choice.     Her fourth child, Charlotte, was one of 139 girls snatched in the early morning of Oct. 10, 1996, from St. Mary's College, a Catholic boarding school in the northern Ugandan town of Aboke.

2003-01-25.title: 2 Frozen Bodies Fall From Jet in Shanghai
2003-01-25.abstract: The Chinese authorities are investigating the deaths of two men who dropped from the undercarriage of an Air France Boeing 777 as the plane approached Shanghai airport after a flight from Paris.    One of the men smashed through the roof of a house, leaving a large gash in the roof.

2003-01-26.title: Israeli Arabs Lose Political Faith as Election Nears
2003-01-26.abstract: Many Israeli Arabs, who represent about 1.2 million of 6.8 million people in Israel, say they have never before felt like such scorned outsiders; their sentiments could well manifest themselves on election day; there seems to be new peak in frustration and sense of alienation among Israeli Arabs; many Israeli Arabs say that while Arab parties in Parliament do not have enough votes to help them that much, they do not believe that predominantly Jewish parties with more power had any interest in addressing wide economic gaps between Arab and Israeli Jews; photo (M)

2003-01-26.title: Making History, South Korea Gives Archenemy a Little Credit
2003-01-26.abstract: New passages in South Korean textbooks have fed a debate about changing relations with North Korea and about the politicization of history during the cold war.

2003-01-26.title: Mafia Trials Show Italy's Mix Of Myth and Mob in Politics
2003-01-26.abstract: Antonio Giuffre, Mafia turncoat, repeatedly testifies that mob has its grip on Italian government, including connections to Prime Min Silvio Berlusconi; forum for accusations is trial of Marcello Dell'Utri, senator in Berlusconi's political party, Forza Italia, as well as prime minister's old friend and business partner; prosectors contend that Dell'Utri was connection between mob and Milan business world; photo (M)

2003-01-26.title: U.S. Is Willing to Stand Alone Against Iraq, Powell Says
2003-01-26.abstract: On the eve of a crucial report by U.N. inspectors, the secretary of state said the U.S. would go to war alone if European allies did not sign on.

2003-01-26.title: Hong Kong Shippers Scramble to Meet New U.S. Cargo Rules
2003-01-26.abstract: United States plans to enforce strict new security rules on Feb 2 for imports by sea in containers, despite complaints from many shippers that they are not ready; rules, drafted in response to Sept 11 attacks, are aimed at reducing risk that nuclear bomb or other bulky weapon of mass destruction could be shipped to United States; but exporters in Hong Kong in particular have been warning of delays and possible chaos at docks and warehouses because of rules (M)

2003-01-27.title: Taiwan Ends a Half-Century Freeze on Flights to China With a Trip to Shanghai
2003-01-27.abstract: Taiwan's China Airlines breaks half-century freeze on commercial aviation across Taiwan Strait, picking up passengers at international airport in Shanghai and returning to Taiwan; flight is first of 16 charter flights to help Taiwanese living in mainland China to return home for Lunar New Year holiday; it is billed as important step toward restoring transportation links between political rivals; China has pushed for direct transport links for several years, but Taiwan has moved slowly to allow people and goods to flow freely to and from mainland, citing security concerns (M)

2003-01-27.title: In One Hand, U.S. Offers Turkey $4 Billion for War Aid; in Other, a Demand for Reform
2003-01-27.abstract: United States offers at least $4 billion to Turkey to compensate for economic damage it might suffer as result of playing active role in American-led coalition in war against Iraq; Recep Tayyip Erdogan, leader of Turkey's governing Justice and Development Party, says $4 billion figure is inadequate to solve Turkey's problems; offer of aid is part of carrot-and-stick approach by Washington to guarantee Turkish participation; Washington wants use of military bases and other facilities that would allow US to use Turkey as staging point for possible attack into northern Iraq; Turkish leadership is worried about domestic political fallout that could result from being seen as too pro-American by largely antiwar Muslim population; Washington has told Prime Min Abdullah Gul that Turkey must within next few days push through critical economic reforms, including radical cuts in public spending, to persuade International Monetary Fund to disburse $1.7 billion installment of fund's package for Turkey; photo (M)

2003-01-27.title: Not Waiting for U.N. Report, Baghdad Assails the U.S.
2003-01-27.abstract: Baghdad's foreign minister launched a scathing verbal attack on the United States hours before the report was delivered in New York.

2003-01-27.title: Tough Talk on Iraq Carries Cost for Blair
2003-01-27.abstract: Prime Minister Tony Blair's moralizing diplomacy, his tough talk on Iraq and his steadfast loyalty to President Bush have gained him acceptance in the United States, but they have cost him popularity at home, opinion polls indicate, and hard-won influence in Europe.

2003-01-27.title: Powell on Iraq: 'We Reserve Our Sovereign Right to Take Military Action'
2003-01-27.abstract: Excerpts from Secretary of State Colin L Powell's speech on Iraq at World Economic Forum; photo (M)

2003-01-28.title: Sharon Leads Likud to Decisive Victory
2003-01-28.abstract: The Israeli prime minister's big win in elections amounted to an endorsement of his tough approach to the conflict with Palestinians.

2003-01-28.title: World Briefing | Africa: Zimbabwe: Aid Workers Questioned
2003-01-28.abstract: Authorities in Zimbabwe question five foreign aid workers, including one American, and accuse them of working as journalists without accreditation; workers are from Lutheran World Federation and write for in-house publication; country's strict media laws require all foreign journalists to be invited before working there (S)

2003-01-28.title: World Briefing | Europe: Vatican: Women's Excommunication Upheld
2003-01-28.abstract: Vatican upholds excommunication of seven women who were 'ordained' in Austria in June 2002 (S)

2003-01-28.title: Crash in Tunnel Kills Two
2003-01-28.abstract: Two trains collide head-on in Alpine tunnel near Italian border in southeast France, killing two people and injuring four seriously (S)

2003-01-28.title: World Briefing | Asia: Japan: $25 Billion In Extra Spending
2003-01-28.abstract: Lower house of Japanese Parliament approves extra $25 billion in new spending on public works projects and job creation (S)

2003-01-29.title: U.S. AND ITS ALLIES FIGHT REBEL FORCE ON AFGHAN PEAKS
2003-01-29.abstract: Some 350 American, European and Afghan forces fought a fierce battle through the night against a large group of rebel fighters in a mountainous region of southeastern Afghanistan, in the heaviest fighting they have encountered in nine months, the American military spokesman said here today.    As many as 80 fighters aligned with the former mujahedeen commander Gulbuddin Hekmatyar, a warlord who once battled Soviet occupation but has now joined forces with the remnants of the Taliban to oust the Americans, were thought to be lodged in caves and mountain hideouts near the border with Pakistan, said the spokesman, Col. Roger King. Those forces fired on Apache helicopters that went to investigate their presence in the Adi Ghar, a mountain range just north of Spinbaldak, the border crossing between the southern Afghan town of Kandahar and the Pakistani city of Quetta.

2003-01-29.title: Ivory Coast Army Rejects Power-Sharing Deal With Rebels
2003-01-29.abstract: A French-brokered peace accord aimed at ending a brutal four-month-long civil war in this country seemed to teeter this evening, as the army rejected key elements of a power-sharing deal with rebel groups and aligned itself with supporters of Ivory Coast's elected government.    The military made its position clear in a letter to Ivory Coast's president, Laurent Gbagbo, rejecting calls for demobilization.

2003-01-29.title: Iraqi Aide Pledges 'Extra Effort' to Cooperate With Inspectors
2003-01-29.abstract: A top adviser to Saddam Hussein pledged tonight to make the ''extra effort'' to cooperate with United Nations weapons inspectors, but offered no dramatic initiative for doing so a day after the inspection team delivered a largely negative report on Iraq's cooperation so far.     ''We have for sure provided complete cooperation in every aspect,'' the adviser, Lt. Gen. Amir Rasheed, a former oil minister, told reporters. ''But as they have said, there is an opportunity for what they call pro-active cooperation. We said, 'O.K., welcome.' Proactive means we are ready as Iraq to facilitate.''

2003-01-29.title: World Briefing: Americas
2003-01-29.abstract: VENEZUELA: Trying To Stop Flight Of Dollars;.

2003-01-29.title: New Delhi Journal; Clean, Modern Subway, Efficiently Built. In India?
2003-01-29.abstract: The trains arrive with a whisper, speak with a computerized voice and at times are driven by women. Passengers board quickly and quietly at stations that are clean and airy, with graceful 30-foot arched ceilings and computerized entryways.    In a city of 14 million people that otherwise tends toward controlled anarchy, it is a pride-inspiring marvel.

2003-01-30.title: India Cabinet Shakeup as Economic Reforms Falter
2003-01-30.abstract: Prime Minister Atal Behari Vajpayee expanded and shuffled his cabinet today in a move seen as intended to aid India's faltering economic reforms ahead of elections late next year.

2003-01-30.title: Arafat Offers Direct Talks With Sharon, Who Declines
2003-01-30.abstract: arafat offers direct talks with sharon, who declines**Yasir Arafat reacts to Prime Min Ariel Sharon's overwhelming victory in Israel's parliamentary elections by offering to begin face-to-face talks immediately; Sharon, who has sought to isolate and oust Arafat as Palestinian leader, rebuffs offer, saying he would speak only to Palestinians 'not involved in terror in any way'; rare, albeit indirect, exchange between old rivals comes as Israel's political factions sort through results of elections, and violence flares again in West Bank and Gaza Strip; photo (M)

2003-01-30.title: Indonesia Clears Top Islamic Militant in Attacks on Christians
2003-01-30.abstract: The verdict could give renewed vigor to Islamic radicals who have been on the defensive in Indonesia since the terrorist blast in Bali last October, analysts said.

2003-01-30.title: Some U.S. Forces in Northern Iraq, Military Chief Says
2003-01-30.abstract: Chairman of Joint Chiefs of Staff Gen Richard B Myers says small numbers of American military forces are now operating in northern Iraq; says Special Operations Forces are in Kurdish areas to work with teams of Central Intelligence Agency operatives who are organizing Kurdish opposition groups; this is first time American military officials have commented publicly on periodic reports of American troops slipping in and out of area; photo (M)

2003-01-30.title: Edward Korry, 81, Is Dead; Falsely Tied to Chile Coup
2003-01-30.abstract: Edward M Korry, United States ambassador to Chile from 1967 to 1971 who was wrongly suspected of helping to plot military coup to prevent Salvador Allende from becoming president, dies at age 81; photo (M)

2003-01-31.title: Ireland to Ban Smoking in Workplace (That Means Pubs, Too)
2003-01-31.abstract: Ireland will ban smoking in all workplaces, including pubs and restaurants, starting in 2004; Health Min Michael Martin's announcement angers tobacco manufacturers and publicans alike, who call ban unenforceable and an infringement of personal liberties (M)

2003-01-31.title: Elections in 2 German States May Be Setback to Schröder
2003-01-31.abstract: German Chancellor Gerhard Schroder used his opposition to war in Iraq to win narrow election in Sept, but opinion polls and commentators say his peace appeal is not working in local election campaign in Lower Saxony that is being seen as referendum on national government; state has one of highest unemployment rates in Germany, and that, not Iraq, is big issue for voters; broad expectation is that Schroder and his party are going to do very poorly in elections there and in Hesse; election losses would seriously erode his authority and prestige only four months after he began his second term (M)

2003-01-31.title: Satellites Said to See Activity At North Korean Nuclear Site
2003-01-31.abstract: American spy satellites detect what appear to be trucks moving North Korea's stockpile of 8,000 nuclear fuel rods out of storage, prompting fears within Bush administration that North Korea is preparing to produce roughly half dozen nuclear weapons; intelligence analysts have seen extensive activity throughout Jan at Yongbyon nuclear complex; satellites cannot see exactly what is being put into trucks, but analysts conclude that it is likely that workers are transporting rods to another site, either to get them out of sight or to move them to reprocessing plant to convert them into bomb-grade plutonium; analysts suspect that North Korea could begin producing bomb-grade plutonium by end of Mar; Bush administration has said nothing publicly about activity, and not shared information widely with allies; some administration officials believe North Korean leader Kim Jong Il hopes to set off crisis to extract concessions from Washington, while others say Pres Bush does not want to distract international attention from Iraq; photos; map (M)

2003-01-31.title: U.N. Estimates Rebuilding Iraq Will Cost $30 Billion
2003-01-31.abstract: United Nations' top development official Mark Malloch Brown concludes costs of rebuilding Iraq after war will run to at least $30 billin in first three years (M)

2003-01-31.title: World Briefing: Europe
2003-01-31.abstract: BELGIUM: Parliament Approves Gay Marriages; BRITAIN: 2 London Subway Lines Closed For Weeks; IRELAND: The Perils Of Power; RUSSIA: Putin Reaches Out To Vatican; SERBIA: Ex-Intelligence Chief Jailed In Attack Plot;.
